reasoning: Based on the FRED time series data, the most recent value of the 10-Year Breakeven Inflation Rate prior to the cutoff date (June 22, 2025) is 2.33 on Friday, June 20. This value represents a short-term local peak, increasing from an early-June low of 2.25. Historically over the past year, values above 2.33 have been relatively rare (occurring mainly in early 2025 with a peak of 2.42, but predominantly staying within the 2.10–2.30 range). Consequently, mean reversion suggests a higher probability that the rate will decline or hover below 2.33 rather than continuing to rise in the immediate future. The statistical trend analysis from FRED indicates roughly a 31% chance of exceeding 2.33 in the very short term (June 29). Furthermore, softer core PCE inflation reports across 2025 (e.g., leveling off around 2.5%) are contributing to a subdued rather than accelerating inflation expectation over the long run. As the forecast horizon extends to July, September, and December 2025, uncertainty increases and the probability fans out toward the long-term historical base rate (closer to 0.5, though maintaining a slight bias below 0.5 given the elevated starting point). Therefore, the estimated probabilities of the breakeven rate strictly exceeding 2.33 are set to steadily increase across the resolution dates from 0.33 to 0.46, representing mean reversion in the short term and growing variance over the longer term.
